title: Semantic Search
lesson:
Semantic search means searching by meaning, not by exact words. "Semantic" just means "to do with meaning". Type "how do I stop paying you" and a good system returns an article titled "Cancelling your subscription" even though the two share almost no words. That is semantic search at work. The older approach, keyword search, only finds documents that contain the literal words you typed.

The two-librarian analogy

Imagine two librarians:

  • The first is a strict index-card matcher. Ask for "car" and she hands you only books with "car" on the spine. Ask for "automobile" and she shrugs, even though the perfect book is right there.
  • The second actually understands you. Say "something about fixing my old motor" and she walks you straight to the auto-repair shelf, no matching words required.

Keyword search is the first librarian. Semantic search is the second.

How the meaning gets measured

The trick behind the second librarian is the Embedding (a list of numbers that captures the meaning of a piece of text). Here is a second analogy: think of a giant map where every sentence is a pin, and sentences with similar meaning sit in the same neighbourhood.

"Refund my order" and "I want my money back" land next door; "the weather in Paris" lands across town. To search, you turn the user's question into its own pin, then ask: which stored pins are nearest?

"Nearest" is measured with cosine similarity — a score from -1 to 1 that compares the direction two number-lists point, ignoring their length. Near 1 means "almost the same meaning"; near 0 means "unrelated". So the recipe is: embed everything once and store it, embed the query, then rank stored items by cosine similarity.

import numpy as np
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

docs = ["Cancelling your subscription",
        "Opening a new account",
        "Shipping times to Canada"]

def embed(texts):
    r = client.embeddings.create(model="text-embedding-3-small", input=texts)
    return [np.array(d.embedding) for d in r.data]

doc_vecs = embed(docs)
q = embed(["how do I stop paying you"])[0]

scores = [q @ d / (np.linalg.norm(q) * np.linalg.norm(d)) for d in doc_vecs]
print(docs[int(np.argmax(scores))])   # -> Cancelling your subscription

In real life you would not loop over every document by hand. A Vector Database stores millions of these vectors and finds the nearest ones in milliseconds using clever shortcuts. Semantic search is the idea; the vector database is the engine that makes it fast.

Retrieve, then re-rank

One embedding-versus-embedding comparison is fast but slightly blunt. The model that produces those vectors is a bi-encoder: it embeds the query and each document separately, so it never sees them side by side. Serious systems add a second stage:

  • The bi-encoder grabs a generous shortlist (say the top 50) cheaply.
  • A cross-encoder (a slower model that reads the query and one document together) re-orders that shortlist and keeps the best 5.

This pattern — fast wide net, then careful judge — is called reranking, and it is the single biggest quality upgrade most people add.

Hybrid search: best of both worlds

Semantic search has one blind spot. If a user types an exact product code like SKU-7741 or a rare surname, meaning-based search can drift to vaguely-similar items and miss the exact match. Keyword search nails those.

So real products often run hybrid search: run both keyword and semantic search, then blend the two ranked lists into one (a common recipe is Reciprocal Rank Fusion). You get the precision of keywords and the understanding of meaning.

Keyword searchSemantic search
Matches exact wordsMatches meaning
Great for codes, names, IDsGreat for vague, natural questions
Misses synonyms (car vs automobile)Finds synonyms easily
No embedding model neededNeeds an embedding model
Fast and cheapSlightly heavier; usually still fast

Where it actually matters

Semantic search is the retrieval half of RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation): before a model answers, you semantically search your own documents and paste the closest chunks into the prompt. It also powers help-centre search, code search, product discovery, and the recall step inside AI Memory Systems. Whenever you want "find things that are about this" rather than "find this exact word", semantic search is the tool. The full plumbing around it lives in Retrieval Pipelines.
takeaways: Search by meaning, not exact words — synonyms still match.
Embed docs + query, then rank by cosine similarity.
Add reranking and hybrid keyword search for real quality.

diagram.caption: Query and documents are turned into embeddings, then cosine similarity ranks which document means the same thing.

quiz.q: A user searches "I forgot my login" but the right FAQ is titled "Resetting your password" — sharing no words. Why does semantic search still find it?
options: Their embeddings point in nearly the same direction, so cosine similarity is high
The system silently rewrites the query to match the title's words
Keyword search matches the word "your" in both
The FAQ was manually tagged with the word "login"
quiz.explain: Semantic search compares meaning via embeddings. "Forgot my login" and "reset password" map to nearby vectors, so cosine similarity is high even with zero shared words.

quiz.q: Your search for the exact part number "SKU-7741" keeps returning vaguely-similar items instead of the exact match. What is the best fix?
options: Switch to a larger embedding model
Add keyword search alongside semantic (hybrid search)
quiz.explain: Exact codes are semantic search's blind spot. Hybrid search blends keyword matching (which nails exact tokens) with semantic recall, covering each method's weakness.

quiz.q: In a retrieve-then-rerank pipeline, what is the cross-encoder's job?
options: Embed every document once so they can be stored in the index
Compress vectors to save memory in the vector database
Read the query and one shortlisted document together to score the match more accurately
Translate the query into multiple languages before searching
quiz.explain: A bi-encoder cheaply fetches a shortlist by embedding things separately. The slower cross-encoder reads the query and a candidate together to re-order that shortlist for quality.

quiz.q: You embedded your documents with one model but, to save cost, embedded incoming queries with a different model. What happens?
options: Results are unaffected — embeddings are universal
Search is faster but uses more memory
Similarity scores become meaningless, so ranking breaks
quiz.explain: Different models place text in different vector spaces. Comparing vectors across models gives nonsense distances, so the whole ranking is unreliable. Always use one model for docs and queries.

mentalModel: Semantic search is a librarian who shelves everything by idea instead of by title: you describe what you vaguely want, and she walks you to the nearest shelf, even if you never said the magic words.
mistakes: Expecting it to find exact codes or names. Searching "SKU-7741" or a rare surname can drift to similar-looking-but-wrong items. For exact tokens, add keyword search alongside it (hybrid search) instead of relying on meaning alone.

Mismatched embedding models. The query and the stored documents must be embedded by the same model and version. Embed your documents with one model and your query with another, and the similarity scores become meaningless.

Skipping reranking and trusting raw similarity. A bi-encoder's top result is often close but not the best. Adding a reranking step (a cross-encoder over the top ~50) dramatically sharpens which answer lands at number one.

Chunking too coarsely. Embedding a whole 50-page document as one vector blurs every topic into mush, so the search returns the right document but you cannot tell where the answer is. Sensible Chunking is what makes results pinpoint-able.

Using the wrong distance metric. Most text embedding models are tuned for cosine similarity. If your index is set to plain straight-line (Euclidean) distance, or you forget to normalise vectors, rankings come out subtly wrong and you blame the model.

Assuming semantic always beats keyword. For short, precise, jargon-heavy queries, plain keyword search can win outright. The reliable default in 2026 is hybrid: blend both and let each cover the other's blind spot.
exercise:
Build a tiny semantic search over 8 short FAQ titles of your own (e.g. "Resetting your password", "Do you ship to Canada?", "Updating your billing card"). Embed all 8 with one embedding model and store the vectors in a plain Python list. Then embed three paraphrased queries that share no words with the right answer, like "I forgot my login", "delivery abroad", and "change the credit card on file". Print the top match for each and confirm the correct FAQ comes back.

Stretch goal: Add a crude keyword search (does the query word appear in the title?) and combine it with your semantic scores into a single hybrid ranking. Then test a query with an exact term like a product code, and show one example where hybrid beats semantic-only.
solution: The win is seeing "I forgot my login" surface the password-reset FAQ with zero shared words, because their embeddings point in nearly the same direction. Two gotchas catch people: embed the query with the same model as the stored titles, and rank by cosine similarity (or normalise first) so vector length doesn't skew things. For the stretch goal, a simple blend is to add a small bonus to any title that contains an exact query word, then sort by the combined score, this is a hand-rolled version of hybrid search. Your product-code query is where it shines: semantic-only may rank a vaguely-similar item first, but the keyword bonus pushes the exact match to the top.
summary: Semantic search finds things by meaning, not exact words: embed everything, embed the query, return the nearest by cosine similarity. A bi-encoder retrieves a fast shortlist; a cross-encoder reranks it for quality; hybrid search blends in keyword matching to catch exact codes and names. It is the retrieval engine behind RAG, help-centre search, and AI memory, just keep one embedding model and the right distance metric.

keyTerms.def: Searching by meaning rather than exact words, so a query finds documents that mean the same thing even if they share no vocabulary.
Traditional search that only returns documents containing the literal words you typed. Great for codes and names, blind to synonyms.
A score from -1 to 1 measuring how much two embeddings point in the same direction. Near 1 means very similar meaning; near 0 means unrelated.
An embedding model that encodes the query and each document separately, fast and scalable, used to fetch a shortlist of candidates.
A slower, more accurate model that reads the query and one document together and scores their match, used to rerank a shortlist.
A second stage that re-orders the cheap shortlist with a more careful model, putting the truly best result at the top.
Running keyword and semantic search together and blending their ranked lists, so you get exact-match precision plus meaning-based recall.
The share of truly relevant items a search actually finds. High recall means few misses; reranking and hybrid search both raise it.
